answer:
The opening of *Widsith* (chapter 14 of *Old English Elegies*) features the scop introducing himself before reciting a long list of kings and tribes.[^1]

> Widsith spoke, unlocked his word-hoard,  
> he who of men had journeyed through most tribes  
> and peoples over the earth; often in the hall  
> he received a memorable treasure. From the Myrgings  
> his lineage arose. He with Ealhhild,  
> the faithful peace-weaver, on his first journey  
> sought the home of the Hreth-king  
> from the east, out of Angeln, the home of Eormanric,  
> the wrathful treaty-breaker. Then he began to speak many things:[^2]  
>   
> "Many men I have heard of who ruled over nations!  
> Every prince must live according to custom,  
> one earl after another ruling the land,  
> he who wishes his throne to thrive.  
> Of these, Hwala was for a time the best,  
> and Alexander the most powerful of all  
> mankind, and he prospered most  
> of those I have heard of across the earth.  
> Attila ruled the Huns, Eormanric the Goths,  
> Becca the Banings, the Burgundians Gifica.  
> Caesar ruled the Greeks and Celic the Finns,  
> Hagena the Holm-Rygs and Heoden the Glomms.  
> Witta ruled the Swæfs, Wada the Hælsings,  
> Meaca the Myrgings, Mearchealf the Hundings.  
> Theodric ruled the Franks, Thyle the Rondings,  
> Breoca the Brondings, Billing the Werns.  
> Oswine ruled the Eows and Gefwulf the Ytes,  
> Fin Folcwalding the Frisian kin.  
> Sigehere ruled the Sea-Danes longest,  
> Hnæf the Hocings, Helm the Wulfings,  
> Wald the Woings, Wod the Thuringians,  
> Sæferth the Sycgs, Ongendtheow the Swedes,  
> Sceafthere the Ymbers, Sceafa the Longbeards,  
> Hun the Hætweras and Holen the Wrosns.  
> Hringweald was the name of the Herefaras' king."[^3]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14 ("Widsith matholade, wordhord onleac,"), translation, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, translation, [passage](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14, translation, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-545201).
